docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
This page covers common ScanReady 1.0 workflow issues and provides solutions for baking, UVs, optimization, memory usage, and realtime preparation.

Scan processing can be demanding because high-poly scans, texture baking, UV generation, and memory usage all depend on the source model and system hardware.

For support, bug reports, or workflow questions, contact:

support.marioschiano3d@gmail.com

docs.title: The ScanReady 1.0 Panel Does Not Appear
docs.text:
Check the following:

  • Make sure the addon is enabled in Edit > Preferences > Add-ons
  • Make sure you are in the 3D Viewport
  • Press N to open the Sidebar
  • Look for the Scan Ready tab
  • In some cases, restarting Blender may be required after enabling the addon

docs.title: One Click Bake Does Not Start
docs.text:
Make sure:

  • A mesh object is selected
  • The selected object is the high-poly scan you want to process
  • Blender is in Object Mode
  • The object is visible and not hidden
  • The scene is not currently running another modal operation

If needed, save the file, restart Blender, and try again.

docs.title: The Preview Mesh Is Too Heavy
docs.text:
If the optimized preview is still too dense:

  • Lower Final Faces
  • Lower Optimize / Reduce
  • Increase cleanup only carefully
  • Create the lowpoly preview again

For VR and videogame assets, the mesh should remain light enough to orbit, inspect, and export comfortably.

docs.title: The Preview Mesh Lost Too Much Detail
docs.text:
If the optimized preview looks too simplified:

  • Increase Final Faces
  • Increase Optimize / Reduce
  • Avoid reducing very thin or delicate objects too aggressively
  • Create the preview again

For important silhouettes, keep enough geometry to preserve the shape.

docs.title: UVs Look Stretched
docs.text:
If the checker pattern shows strong stretching:

  • Try a different UV method
  • Lower or raise Smart UV Angle
  • Increase UV island separation with UV Padding
  • Use a more detailed UV preset
  • Generate UVs again

Clean UVs are important for good baked textures.

docs.title: The Bake Misses Details
docs.text:
If parts of the scan detail are missing in the baked texture:

  • Increase Cage Extrusion slightly
  • Use Auto Cage Extrusion
  • Enable Show Cage and inspect the cage
  • Check the cage preview and increase Cage Extrusion if the cage does not fully cover the high-poly source
  • Increase Texture Size if the bake is too low resolution
  • Make sure the original high-poly object is still available

Use the smallest cage value that captures the details cleanly.

docs.title: Why Does the Baked Texture Look Low Quality?
docs.text:
Bake quality depends on several factors:

  • Texture resolution
  • Polygon density
  • UV space usage
  • Number of materials

docs.title: Increase Texture Resolution
docs.text:
Try increasing the texture resolution first.

Example:

  • 1024 → low detail
  • 2048 → standard quality
  • 4096 → high quality
  • 8192 → very high detail

Higher resolutions increase memory usage.

docs.title: Increase Polygon Density
docs.text:
If the optimized mesh is too aggressive, important details may be lost before baking.

Try:

  • Increasing Final Faces
  • Using a less aggressive Optimize / Reduce value

docs.title: Use Multiple Materials
docs.text:
If increasing texture resolution is still not enough, the asset may require multiple materials.

Each material receives its own texture space, allowing much higher detail preservation across the model.

Increasing polygon density alone is not always the best solution.

Very dense meshes can become heavier to process, slower to bake, and more difficult to use in realtime applications.

For this reason, ScanReady can recommend an appropriate number of bake materials depending on the complexity and size of the scan.

Example:

  • 1 material → lower texture detail
  • 2 materials → improved texture quality
  • 4 materials → significantly higher texture detail

Using multiple materials is often a better solution than simply increasing polygon density.

This is especially useful for:

  • Large scans
  • Environment assets
  • Museum objects
  • Complex photogrammetry assets
  • Fine surface details

docs.title: The Bake Captures Wrong Areas
docs.text:
If the bake includes details from the wrong part of the model:

  • Reduce Cage Extrusion
  • Inspect the cage preview
  • Make sure objects are not overlapping
  • Hide or move unrelated objects if needed
  • Bake again

A cage that is too large can project unwanted nearby surfaces.

docs.title: Textures Are Not Saved
docs.text:
Check:

  • Save Images is enabled
  • Output Folder is valid
  • The Blender file has been saved if you use a relative path like //bake/
  • You have permission to write in the selected folder
  • The bake completed successfully

Relative paths are saved next to the current .blend file.

After a successful bake, use Bake Folder in Step 3 to open the latest texture folder. If the button is not visible yet, run a bake with Save Images enabled first.

docs.title: Why Is the Bake Very Slow?
docs.text:
Baking can be slow with large scans or high resolution textures.

To speed it up:

  • Lower Texture Size
  • Lower Bake Samples
  • Reduce the number of bake materials if possible
  • Disable maps you do not need
  • Use a smaller final mesh when appropriate

High resolution Normal and AO maps can increase bake time.

docs.title: Blender Runs Out of Memory
docs.text:
Large scans can use a lot of memory during baking.

Try:

  • Enable Safe Memory Bake
  • Enable Force CPU Baking if GPU memory is limited
  • Lower Texture Size
  • Lower the number of bake materials
  • Close other heavy applications
  • Save and restart Blender before baking

Force CPU Baking is usually slower, but it can be safer on low VRAM systems.

ScanReady 1.0 automatically enables Force CPU Baking when the number of bake materials is set to 2 or more.

For single-material baking, GPU baking can still be used when available.

docs.title: Normal Map Looks Too Strong or Too Weak
docs.text:
Adjust Normal Strength.

This changes the strength of the Normal Map node in the final material.

It affects the material appearance, not the baked normal image itself.

If the original high-poly material has a linked normal texture, ScanReady transfers that normal map to the new UV layout.

If no normal texture is linked, ScanReady generates the normal information from the high-poly geometry.

docs.title: Roughness Information Is Missing
docs.text:
ScanReady can only transfer roughness information when the original high-poly material already contains a roughness texture or usable roughness input.

If no roughness information exists in the original material, no roughness transfer can be generated automatically.

Check the original material node tree and verify that roughness information is available before baking.

docs.title: Ambient Occlusion Looks Too Dark
docs.text:
Try:

  • Lowering AO strength in your final material
  • Reducing AO Distance if automatic distance is disabled
  • Using more controlled AO settings
  • Checking whether the AO source is appropriate for the asset

AO should add depth, not hide scan details.

docs.title: Why Does the Bake Contain Black or Missing Areas?
docs.text:
If the baked texture contains black areas, missing details, or incorrect projections, the cage extrusion is usually too small.

In this case, some parts of the high-poly scan are not being reached correctly during baking.

Black areas can also happen when parts of the optimized lowpoly mesh sit in front of or behind the high-poly source, so the bake rays do not hit the expected surface.

To fix this:

  • Enable Show Cage
  • Increase the Cage Extrusion value
  • Inspect the cage around the model before baking
  • Check the cage preview before baking
  • Increase Cage Extrusion slightly if the cage does not fully cover the high-poly source

The cage should fully surround the high-poly surface.

In ScanReady 1.0, the cage preview turns green when the cage is large enough to properly cover the scan surface.

If some areas are still missing, increase the extrusion value slightly and check the cage again.

Once the cage looks correct, run the bake again to verify whether the missing or black areas have been fixed.

Use the smallest value that completely covers the scan without capturing unwanted nearby surfaces.

docs.title: Which Adaptive Reduce Preset should I use?
docs.text:
Adaptive Reduce changes how ScanReady distributes mesh reduction across the scan before UVs and baking.

Preset Best For Result Balanced Most scans Good general-purpose reduction behavior. Use this first. Preserve Details Complex scans with sculptural forms, folds, engravings, damage, or small details Protects important detail areas more strongly. Flat Surfaces Objects with broad simple surfaces, panels, walls, floors, or flatter architectural areas Reduces simple areas more aggressively to save polygons. Hard Surface Vehicles, props, machinery, and hard-surface scans Uses a faster approximate pass and protects only stronger normal breaks.

If the optimized preview loses important detail, try Preserve Details and create the preview again.

If the preview keeps too much geometry on simple areas, try Flat Surfaces and create the preview again.

For vehicles or hard-surface scans, try Hard Surface and check the silhouette and main panel breaks.

docs.title: Best First Fix
docs.text:
If the result is not good, use this order:

  1. Check the lowpoly preview
  2. Check UVs with the checker view
  3. Check the cage preview
  4. Bake only Base Color first
  5. Add Normal, Roughness, and AO after the Base Color works
  6. Increase texture resolution only when the workflow is correct

This makes problems easier to isolate.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
ScanReady 1.0 is designed for workflows where high-poly scans need to become lighter, cleaner, and easier to use.

Raw 3D scans are often too heavy for realtime production. They may look detailed, but they can be difficult to move, export, render, or use inside VR and game engines.

ScanReady 1.0 helps convert those scans into optimized baked assets while preserving the visual identity of the original capture.

docs.title: VR and AR
docs.text:
VR and AR experiences require stable performance.

A very dense scan can be too heavy for smooth interaction, especially on standalone headsets or realtime viewers.

ScanReady 1.0 helps prepare scanned models for VR and AR by:

  • Reducing polygon count.
  • Creating a lighter mesh.
  • Baking visual detail into textures.
  • Supporting Normal and Ambient Occlusion maps.
  • Helping keep the asset easier to render.
  • Making the model more practical for interactive scenes.

This is useful for virtual museums, training simulations, cultural heritage experiences, product visualization, and immersive environments.

docs.title: Videogames
docs.text:
Game engines need assets that balance visual quality and performance.

A raw scan with millions of polygons is rarely suitable for direct use in a videogame.

ScanReady 1.0 helps prepare game-ready assets by:

  • Creating an optimized lowpoly version.
  • Generating UVs.
  • Preparing a bake cage.
  • Baking texture information from the high-poly scan.
  • Saving texture maps for external workflows.
  • Keeping detail in textures instead of heavy geometry.

This makes scanned props, environment pieces, and real-world objects easier to bring into a game pipeline.

docs.title: Realtime Visualization
docs.text:
Realtime visualization requires assets that can be inspected, moved, and rendered smoothly.

ScanReady 1.0 is useful when you need to show scanned objects in:

  • Interactive product viewers.
  • Architecture previews.
  • Web-based 3D viewers.
  • Blender realtime scenes.
  • Realtime rendering tools.
  • Lightweight review scenes.

The addon helps reduce scene weight while keeping the object visually recognizable.

docs.title: Cultural Heritage
docs.text:
Cultural heritage scans often contain valuable surface detail, but the raw data can be very heavy.

ScanReady 1.0 can help create lighter versions for:

  • Virtual exhibitions.
  • Museum previews.
  • Educational experiences.
  • Online collections.
  • Interactive archives.
  • VR historical reconstructions.

The goal is not to replace the original scan archive.

The goal is to create a practical version that can be used for presentation, interaction, and realtime exploration.

docs.title: Photogrammetry Assets
docs.text:
Photogrammetry often produces detailed but dense meshes.

ScanReady 1.0 helps process photogrammetry models by:

  • Simplifying the captured geometry.
  • Creating usable UVs.
  • Baking visual information from the source scan.
  • Saving texture maps.
  • Preparing assets for further editing or export.

This can be useful for rocks, statues, props, terrain pieces, objects, ruins, artifacts, and real-world captured surfaces.

docs.title: Large Scan Cleanup
docs.text:
Large scans can slow down Blender and make editing difficult.

ScanReady 1.0 helps create a more manageable asset that is easier to:

  • Orbit in the viewport.
  • Inspect.
  • Bake.
  • Export.
  • Use in scenes.
  • Share with collaborators.

The optimized result can be used as a lighter production asset while keeping the original high-poly scan as the source.

docs.title: Typical Workflow
docs.text:
A typical ScanReady 1.0 use case looks like this:

  1. Import or open a high-poly scan in Blender.
  2. Select the scan object.
  3. Use ONE CLICK BAKE for a fast automatic result.
  4. Inspect the optimized mesh and baked textures.
  5. Use manual steps if the asset needs more control.
  6. Export the final asset for VR, games, realtime visualization, or presentation.

docs.title: Main Benefit
docs.text:
ScanReady 1.0 helps bridge the gap between scan quality and realtime usability.

It turns heavy captured models into assets that are easier to manage, easier to bake, and easier to use in production.

The result is a lighter asset that keeps the look of the scan without carrying all the original geometry weight.

docs.title: Why ScanReady?
docs.text:
Photogrammetry scans are detailed, but often too heavy for VR, games, realtime viewers, and interactive projects.

ScanReady helps you turn those scans into optimized baked assets directly inside Blender.

docs.title: What ScanReady Solves
docs.text:
• Heavy polygon counts.
  • Unoptimized scan topology.
  • Missing UV-ready lowpoly.
  • Manual cage setup.
  • Repetitive bake preparation.
  • Slow scan-to-realtime workflow.

docs.title: Smart Adaptive Optimization
docs.text:
Traditional mesh reduction often applies the same optimization uniformly across the entire model.

This can unnecessarily preserve dense geometry in flat areas while damaging important surface details in more complex regions.

ScanReady 1.0 uses adaptive optimization to analyze the surface and preserve visually important details while simplifying flatter or less detailed areas more aggressively.

The result is a cleaner and more efficient lowpoly asset that keeps much more of the original scan quality where it matters most.

docs.title: Built for Photogrammetry and Game-Ready Assets
docs.text:
ScanReady is designed to convert dense high-poly scans into optimized, game-ready assets directly inside Blender.

Instead of spending hours manually cleaning meshes, generating UVs, creating cages, and baking textures, ScanReady automates the workflow into a faster and more efficient pipeline.

docs.title: Better Texture Space Usage
docs.text:
ScanReady 1.0 does not only optimize polygon density.

The addon also helps improve UV space efficiency and bake preparation for cleaner and more detailed realtime textures.

Optimized UV packing helps preserve texture detail while reducing unnecessary texture waste.

docs.title: The Problem with Traditional Workflows
docs.text:
Photogrammetry scans are often:

  • Extremely heavy
  • Difficult to optimize
  • Hard to unwrap correctly
  • Slow to bake
  • Unstable on low VRAM systems

A single scan can easily contain millions of polygons, making manual optimization time-consuming and frustrating.

docs.title: What ScanReady Solves
docs.text:
✅ Mesh optimization

Automatically reduce dense geometry while preserving important shapes and details.

✅ Smart UV workflow

Generate clean UVs optimized for baking and texture usage.

✅ Automatic cage generation

No need to manually create cages for baking.

✅ Multi-material baking

Supports complex scan assets with multiple materials.

✅ Memory-safe baking

Designed to work safely even on systems with limited VRAM.

✅ One-click workflow

From scan to game-ready asset with minimal setup.

docs.title: Designed for Blender Artists
docs.text:
ScanReady works directly inside Blender and integrates into existing workflows without external software.

Perfect for:

  • Photogrammetry artists
  • Environment artists
  • Game developers
  • Asset creators
  • VR and AR workflows
  • Real-time applications

docs.title: From Millions of Polygons to Realtime Assets
docs.text:
ScanReady helps transform heavy scans into optimized assets suitable for:

  • Unreal Engine
  • Unity
  • Real-time rendering
  • VR applications
  • AR experiences
  • Game production

while preserving important visual surface details through textures and adaptive optimization.

docs.title: Why Not Manual Optimization?
docs.text:
Manual workflows often require:

  • Decimation setup
  • UV fixing
  • Cage tweaking
  • Baking setup
  • Material cleanup
  • Repeated testing

ScanReady automates these repetitive technical tasks so artists can spend more time focusing on visual quality and creative work.

docs.title: Built for Speed
docs.text:
ScanReady is designed to reduce the technical complexity of scan optimization workflows.

Tasks that normally require multiple manual steps — mesh cleanup, optimization, UV generation, cage setup, and baking — can be prepared significantly faster inside a single integrated workflow.

The goal is simple:

Reduce technical friction and accelerate scan-to-realtime asset creation.

docs.title: Automated Workflow
docs.text:
ScanReady combines optimization, UV generation, cage preparation, and baking into a more automated workflow designed specifically for scanned assets.

Instead of manually configuring multiple technical steps, artists can focus on preparing cleaner realtime assets more efficiently.

docs.title: Example Workflow
docs.text:
1. Import high-poly scan
  2. Preview and optimize mesh
  3. Generate UVs and cage
  4. Bake textures
  5. Export game-ready asset

docs.title: ScanReady Philosophy
docs.text:
Fast.

Clean.

Game-ready.

Built for artists who want efficient scan optimization directly inside Blender.
